title: Web storage and serialization

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Web storage and serialization; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Web storage and serialization
sources.claim: Web storage stores origin-scoped strings synchronously, while IndexedDB supports structured asynchronous storage; neither is a trusted secret vault.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Web storage stores origin-scoped strings synchronously, while IndexedDB supports structured asynchronous storage; neither is a trusted secret vault.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A preference is JSON-validated after localStorage read and falls back safely when missing or corrupt. Your goal is to implement resilient local preference storage without treating it as trusted input. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: DOM, events and forms; Promises, async work and HTTP; Object records and property access; Errors, throw and recovery boundaries. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Web storage stores origin-scoped strings synchronously, while IndexedDB supports structured asynchronous storage; neither is a trusted secret vault. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Quotas, eviction, private mode, cross-tab changes, serialization, and XSS exposure affect reliability. This draft therefore rejects: localStorage is encrypted and safe for long-lived API keys because it belongs to one origin.

includes: Persist small non-sensitive preferences with versioned parsing and quota or corruption handling.
Implement resilient local preference storage without treating it as trusted input.
excludes: Quotas, eviction, private mode, cross-tab changes, serialization, and XSS exposure affect reliability.

outcomes: Implement resilient local preference storage without treating it as trusted input.

examples.title: Web storage and serialization: concrete case
examples.situation: A preference is JSON-validated after localStorage read and falls back safely when missing or corrupt.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A preference is JSON-validated after localStorage read and falls back safely when missing or corrupt.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat browser storage as script-readable and untrusted; do not store sensitive long-lived credentials there.
examples.result: The result follows because Treat browser storage as script-readable and untrusted; do not store sensitive long-lived credentials there.

examples.title: Web storage and serialization: boundary case
examples.situation: Quotas, eviction, private mode, cross-tab changes, serialization, and XSS exposure affect reliability.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Quotas, eviction, private mode, cross-tab changes, serialization, and XSS exposure affect reliability.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: localStorage is encrypted and safe for long-lived API keys because it belongs to one origin.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Treat browser storage as script-readable and untrusted; do not store sensitive long-lived credentials there.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement resilient local preference storage without treating it as trusted input. Keep this boundary in view: Quotas, eviction, private mode, cross-tab changes, serialization, and XSS exposure affect reliability.
